lessons.title: Lesson 1: What Is a Computer Network
lessons.overview: A computer network is a group of devices connected together so they can communicate and share resources.
lessons.definition: When devices such as computers, phones, printers, or servers are connected, they can send and receive data. These connections can be wired using cables or wireless using Wi-Fi. Networks allow devices to work together instead of operating alone.
examples: You can send files between computers
Multiple users can use the same printer
Devices can access the internet
Your home Wi-Fi is a simple network. Your phone, laptop, smart TV, and tablet all connect to the same router and communicate through it.
keyPoints: Networks can be wired or wireless

lessons.title: Lesson 2: Why Networks Are Important
lessons.overview: Computer networks are the foundation of modern communication and digital services.
lessons.definition: Almost everything we do online depends on networks. When you browse websites, send emails, or watch videos, your device is communicating with other computers through networks. Businesses rely on networks to share files, run applications, and connect employees. Schools use networks for learning platforms and research. Banks use networks for financial transactions. Networks make it possible for millions of devices around the world to communicate instantly.
examples: When you open a website, your computer sends a request through the network to a server that stores that website. The server then sends the website data back to your device.
keyPoints: They support internet services and online applications
Businesses and organizations depend on networks

lessons.title: Lesson 4: Client vs Server
lessons.overview: In many networks, devices have different roles. Two common roles are clients and servers.
lessons.definition: A client is a device that requests services or information. Examples include laptops, smartphones, and desktop computers. A server is a powerful computer that provides services, data, or applications to clients. When a client needs something, it sends a request to a server. The server processes that request and sends back the response. This model is used by most internet services.
examples: When you open a website:
Your computer acts as the client
The website's hosting machine acts as the server
The server sends the webpage to your browser
keyPoints: Most internet systems follow the client-server model

lessons.title: Lesson 1: Local Area Network (LAN)
lessons.overview: A Local Area Network is a network that connects devices within a small physical area.
lessons.definition: A LAN usually exists in places like homes, offices, schools, or small buildings. Devices inside a LAN are connected using Ethernet cables or Wi-Fi and communicate through a router or switch. LANs are fast because the devices are close to each other and the network is controlled locally.
examples: Your home Wi-Fi network where your phone, laptop, and smart TV connect to the same router is a LAN.
keyPoints: A LAN covers a small area

lessons.title: Lesson 2: Wide Area Network (WAN)
lessons.overview: A Wide Area Network connects multiple smaller networks across large geographic areas.
lessons.definition: WANs allow networks in different cities or countries to communicate with each other. They rely on infrastructure provided by internet service providers. The largest WAN in the world is the internet.
examples: When a company has offices in different cities, they use a WAN to connect all their office networks.
keyPoints: WAN connects networks across large distances
Used by companies and internet providers
The internet is the largest WAN

lessons.title: Lesson 3: Metropolitan Area Network (MAN)
lessons.overview: A Metropolitan Area Network connects networks within a city or large urban area.
lessons.definition: MANs are larger than LANs but smaller than WANs. They are often operated by governments, universities, or telecommunications companies. They provide high-speed connections between buildings in the same city.
examples: A city government network connecting public offices, libraries, and schools.
keyPoints: Covers a city or metropolitan area
Larger than LAN but smaller than WAN
Often used by institutions and municipalities

lessons.title: Lesson 4: Personal Area Network (PAN)
lessons.overview: A Personal Area Network connects devices around a single person.
lessons.definition: PANs are small networks used for short-range communication between personal devices. They usually use technologies like Bluetooth or Wi-Fi Direct. These networks allow devices to exchange data easily without needing a large network.
examples: Connecting your phone to wireless headphones using Bluetooth.

lessons.title: Lesson 5: Public vs Private Networks
lessons.overview: Networks can be classified based on who is allowed to access them.
lessons.definition: A public network is open and accessible to many users. The internet is the most common example. A private network is restricted and used internally within an organization or home. Private networks provide better security because access is controlled.
examples: A company internal network used only by employees is a private network.

lessons.title: Lesson 1: Router
lessons.overview: A router is a device that connects different networks and directs data traffic between them.
lessons.definition: Routers examine the destination address of data packets and decide the best path to send them. In most homes and offices, the router connects the local network to the internet.
examples: Your home router allows all your devices to share a single internet connection.
keyPoints: Commonly used to connect to the internet

lessons.title: Lesson 2: Switch
lessons.overview: A switch connects multiple devices within the same local network.
lessons.definition: Switches receive data from one device and send it directly to the correct destination device within the network. This helps improve network efficiency because data is not broadcast to every device.
examples: In an office network, computers are often connected to a switch using Ethernet cables.
keyPoints: Sends data to the correct device

lessons.title: Lesson 3: Modem
lessons.overview: A modem allows a network to connect to an internet service provider.
lessons.definition: The modem converts signals from your ISP into a format your network devices can understand. In many homes, the modem and router are combined into one device.
examples: Your internet modem connects your home network to your internet provider.
keyPoints: Connects your network to the ISP

lessons.title: Lesson 4: Wireless Access Point
lessons.overview: A wireless access point allows devices to connect to a network using Wi-Fi.
lessons.definition: Access points extend wireless coverage and allow many devices to connect without cables. They are commonly used in offices, schools, and large buildings.
examples: A coffee shop Wi-Fi system uses access points to provide internet access to customers.

lessons.title: Lesson 5: Firewall
lessons.overview: A firewall protects a network from unauthorized access.
lessons.definition: Firewalls monitor incoming and outgoing traffic and block suspicious or harmful connections. They act as a security barrier between trusted internal networks and untrusted external networks.
examples: A company firewall prevents hackers from accessing internal company systems.

lessons.title: Lesson 1: Introduction to Network Topology
lessons.overview: Network topology describes how devices are arranged and connected within a network.
lessons.definition: The layout of a network affects how devices communicate and how reliable the network is. Some topologies are simple and inexpensive, while others are designed for reliability and fault tolerance. Understanding topology helps network engineers design efficient networks.
examples: In many office networks, all computers connect to a central switch. This layout is known as a star topology.
keyPoints: Network topology describes the layout of a network

lessons.title: Lesson 2: Star Topology
lessons.overview: Star topology is one of the most common network designs.
lessons.definition: In this topology, every device connects to a central device such as a switch or hub. All communication between devices passes through this central point. If one device fails, the rest of the network continues working. However, if the central device fails, the network stops functioning.
examples: Most modern office networks use star topology with a central switch.
keyPoints: Devices connect to a central device
Failure of the central device affects the whole network

lessons.title: Lesson 3: Bus Topology
lessons.overview: Bus topology connects all devices using a single communication cable.
lessons.definition: All devices share the same transmission line. When one device sends data, it travels along the cable and can be received by other devices. This design is simple but not commonly used today because it has performance and reliability limitations.
examples: Early computer networks often used bus topology.

lessons.title: Lesson 4: Ring Topology
lessons.overview: In a ring topology, devices are connected in a circular structure.
lessons.definition: Each device connects to two other devices, forming a closed loop. Data travels around the ring until it reaches the destination. Although this design ensures orderly communication, a break in the ring can disrupt the network.
examples: Some early networking systems used ring-based communication.
keyPoints: A single break can affect communication

lessons.title: Lesson 5: Mesh Topology
lessons.overview: Mesh topology connects devices using multiple paths.
lessons.definition: Each device may have connections to several other devices. This design improves reliability because if one connection fails, data can take another path. Mesh networks are often used in critical systems where uptime is very important.
examples: Large internet infrastructure networks often use mesh connections.
keyPoints: Used in large and critical networks

lessons.title: Lesson 1: What Is an IP Address
lessons.overview: An IP address is a unique identifier assigned to every device on a network.
lessons.definition: Just like houses have street addresses, devices use IP addresses so they can send and receive data. Without IP addresses, devices would not know where to send information.
examples: When you visit a website, your device sends a request to the IP address of the server hosting that website.
keyPoints: Every connected device has an IP address

lessons.title: Lesson 2: IPv4 Address Structure
lessons.overview: IPv4 is the most widely used IP addressing system.
lessons.definition: An IPv4 address is written as four numbers separated by periods. Each number ranges from 0 to 255. Example format: 192.168.1.1. Each section of the address represents part of the network identification system.

lessons.title: Lesson 3: Public vs Private IP Addresses
lessons.overview: IP addresses can be classified as public or private.
lessons.definition: A public IP address is used on the internet and is assigned by an internet service provider. A private IP address is used inside local networks such as homes or offices. Private addresses allow internal communication while protecting devices from direct exposure to the internet.
keyPoints: Public IP addresses exist on the internet
Private IP addresses exist inside local networks

lessons.title: Lesson 4: Static vs Dynamic IP
lessons.overview: IP addresses can be assigned in different ways.
lessons.definition: A static IP address is manually assigned and does not change. A dynamic IP address is automatically assigned by a network service and may change over time. Dynamic addressing is commonly used in home networks.
keyPoints: Static IP addresses stay the same

lessons.title: Lesson 5: Introduction to Subnetting
lessons.overview: Subnetting divides a large network into smaller segments.
lessons.definition: Breaking a network into smaller parts helps improve performance and organization. It also allows network administrators to control traffic and manage devices more efficiently.

lessons.title: Lesson 1: What Is a MAC Address
lessons.overview: A MAC address is a unique hardware identifier assigned to a network interface.
lessons.definition: Unlike IP addresses, MAC addresses are built into network devices by manufacturers. They help devices identify each other inside local networks.

lessons.title: Lesson 2: IP Address vs MAC Address
lessons.overview: IP and MAC addresses both identify devices but serve different purposes.
lessons.definition: An IP address identifies a device across networks. A MAC address identifies a device within a local network. Together they help deliver data correctly.

lessons.title: Lesson 3: Address Resolution Protocol
lessons.overview: Devices use ARP to match IP addresses with MAC addresses.
lessons.definition: When a device wants to send data to another device in the same network, it must know the destination MAC address. ARP helps discover that MAC address.
keyPoints: Connects IP addresses to MAC addresses

lessons.title: Lesson 1: Domain Name System
lessons.overview: The Domain Name System converts domain names into IP addresses.
lessons.definition: Humans prefer using names like google.com, but computers communicate using IP addresses. DNS acts like a directory that translates names into addresses.
keyPoints: Makes the internet easier to use

lessons.title: Lesson 2: How Domain Names Work
lessons.definition: When you type a website address, your computer sends a request to a DNS server. The DNS server finds the corresponding IP address and returns it to your device. Your device can then contact the correct server.

lessons.title: Lesson 3: Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol
lessons.overview: DHCP automatically assigns IP addresses to devices.
lessons.definition: Instead of manually configuring every device, DHCP provides IP addresses and other network settings automatically when a device connects to a network.

lessons.title: Lesson 1: What Is a Network Protocol
lessons.definition: Protocols are rules that define how devices communicate and exchange information across networks. Different protocols are used for different types of communication.

lessons.title: Lesson 2: Common Internet Protocols
lessons.definition: Examples include: HTTP for web pages, HTTPS for secure websites, FTP for file transfer, SSH for remote access. These protocols define how data is transmitted.

lessons.title: Lesson 3: What Are Ports
lessons.definition: Ports allow multiple services to run on the same device. Each service uses a different port number so incoming data can reach the correct application.

lessons.title: Lesson 4: Well Known Ports
lessons.definition: Examples: 80 for HTTP, 443 for HTTPS, 21 for FTP, 22 for SSH. These numbers help systems recognize the type of service being requested.

lessons.title: Lesson 1: Introduction to the OSI Model
lessons.definition: This model divides network communication into seven layers. Each layer performs a specific role in the data communication process.

lessons.title: Lesson 2: The Seven Layers Explained
lessons.definition: The layers include: Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, Application. Each layer helps organize network functions.

lessons.title: Lesson 3: Introduction to the TCP/IP Model
lessons.definition: The TCP/IP model is the practical framework used by the internet. It groups network communication into four layers.

lessons.title: Lesson 4: Comparing OSI and TCP/IP
lessons.definition: Both models describe how communication works in networks, but the TCP/IP model is more commonly used in real-world networking.

lessons.title: Lesson 1: Ping
lessons.definition: Ping is a basic tool used to test whether two devices can communicate across a network.

lessons.title: Lesson 2: Traceroute
lessons.definition: Traceroute shows the path that data takes across networks to reach its destination.

lessons.title: Lesson 3: Packet Analysis
lessons.definition: Tools like Wireshark allow engineers to inspect network traffic and analyze packets.

lessons.title: Lesson 4: Network Simulation
lessons.definition: Simulation tools like Cisco Packet Tracer allow students to practice networking concepts in a virtual environment.
